1: After Nebuchadnezzar king of Babylon had taken into exile from Jerusalem Jeconiah the son of Jehoiakim, king of Judah, together with the officials of Judah, the craftsmen, and the metal workers, and had brought them to Babylon, YHWH showed me this vision: behold, two baskets of figs placed before the Temple of YHWH. Chapter 24 is dated to after the first Babylonian deportation of 597 BC, when Jehoiachin and the first group of exiles (including Ezekiel and the skilled artisans who made the city defensible) were taken to Babylon. The vision of the two fig baskets is one of the most theologically counterintuitive in Jeremiah: those who have been deported (seemingly punished) are the good figs; those who remain in Jerusalem (seemingly preserved) are the bad figs.
2: One basket had very good figs, like first-ripe figs, but the other basket had very bad figs, so bad that they could not be eaten. The contrast is absolute: very good (tovot me'od) versus very bad (ra'ot me'od), so bad as to be inedible. The two baskets represent two populations — not two categories of personal righteousness but two groups defined by their relationship to YHWH's covenant judgment: those who accepted the exile as YHWH's discipline and those who resisted or evaded it. The quality of the figs is not earned but determined by YHWH's sovereign disposition toward each group.
3: And YHWH said to me, What do you see, Jeremiah? I said, Figs, the good figs very good, and the bad figs very bad, so bad that they cannot be eaten. The dialogical form (what do you see?) appears in the call narrative (Jeremiah 1:11, 13 — what do you see?) and in Amos 7–8 (amos: what do you see?). The question-answer form involves the prophet actively in the revelation process: he names what he sees before receiving the interpretation. This participatory reception marks authentic prophetic experience.
4: Then the word of YHWH came to me — the interpretive oracle follows the vision. The two-stage structure (vision + word) is standard in prophetic literature. The interpretation will make the theological point that the natural reading of the situation (those deported are punished; those remaining are preserved) is the exact reverse of YHWH's actual assessment.
5: Thus says YHWH, the God of Israel: Like these good figs, so I will regard as good the exiles from Judah, whom I have sent away from this place to the land of the Chaldeans. The exile is reframed: YHWH sent the exiles — they were not merely conquered but providentially directed. The theological claim is radical: the Babylonian deportation of 597 BC was YHWH's act of grace disguised as judgment. Those removed to Babylon are under YHWH's good disposition (akkirem letovah, I will acknowledge them for good). The exile is not abandonment but a form of covenant protection — removing the remnant from the coming total destruction of Jerusalem.
6: I will set my eyes on them for good, and I will bring them back to this land. I will build them up, and not tear them down; I will plant them, and not uproot them. The restoration promise for the good-fig exiles uses the construction/planting vocabulary from Jeremiah's call (Jeremiah 1:10) — but now entirely in the positive register: build… not tear down; plant… not uproot. The six terms of Jeremiah 1:10 (pluck up, break down, destroy, overthrow, build, plant) are reduced here to the two final positive terms. The work of judgment is complete; the work of restoration can begin.
7: I will give them a heart to know me, that I am YHWH, and they shall be my people and I will be their God, for they shall return to me with their whole heart. The good-fig exiles will receive a heart to know (lev lada'at) YHWH — a phrase that anticipates the New Covenant of Jeremiah 31:33 (I will put my law within them and write it on their hearts). The covenant formula they shall be my people and I will be their God is the center of the entire covenant tradition, from Sinai through the prophets to Revelation 21:3. Whole heart (lev shalem): total covenant orientation, the undivided heart that Deuteronomy 6:5 commanded (love YHWH with all your heart).
8: But like the bad figs that are so bad they cannot be eaten, so will I treat Zedekiah the king of Judah, his officials, the remnant of Jerusalem who remain in this land, and those who dwell in the land of Egypt. The bad figs include three groups: Zedekiah and his court (who remained in Jerusalem after 597), the Jerusalem remnant (those not deported), and the Egyptian community (those who fled to Egypt rather than submit to Babylon). The theological logic: all three groups avoided the exile — either by remaining in the land or by fleeing to Egypt — and YHWH regards all three as bad figs. Evasion of YHWH's appointed judgment is itself a form of covenant rejection.
9: I will make them a horror, a calamity, to all the kingdoms of the earth, to be a reproach, a byword, a taunt, and a curse in all the places where I shall drive them. The bad-fig fate is a comprehensive shame-catalogue: horror, calamity, reproach, byword, taunt, curse. Each term represents a different dimension of public disgrace in the ancient Near East. A byword (mashal) means one's name becomes a proverb of misfortune. A curse (qelalah) means one's fate is invoked when threatening others with the worst possible outcome. The covenant curses of Deuteronomy 28:37 (become a byword, a taunt among all the nations) are precisely fulfilled.
10: And I will send sword, famine, and pestilence upon them, until they shall be utterly destroyed from the land that I gave to them and their fathers. The final verse applies the judgment triad to the bad figs: sword, famine, pestilence — the same three instruments announced throughout Jeremiah (Jeremiah 14:12; Jeremiah 15:2; Jeremiah 21:7–9). Utterly destroyed from the land: the bad-fig population loses all covenant standing in the covenant land. The land, YHWH's gift to the fathers, will be stripped from those who refused to submit to YHWH's judgment. The chapter ends on this note of total judgment for evasion — making the theological point that accepting YHWH's correction leads to restoration, while evading it leads to destruction.
